data.title: CommScope: More Parts Involved In The Sum-Of-The-Parts?
data.description:
Less than three weeks ago, I concluded that investors in CommScope (COMM) were awaiting the closing of the deal with Amphenol (APH) and, related to that, remarks about a special dividend payable as well.

This was actually the second deal with Amphenol, that of a $10.5 billion sale of the Connectivity & Cable Solutions division, as announced last summer. This was a huge and transformative deal, leaving an overleveraged business with a complicated preferred equity position flushed with cash, and frankly gave rise to compelling sum-of-the-parts analysis.

From Overleveraged To Flushed With Cash

Originally founded in 1976, CommScope went public again in 2013 after a private equity adventure in between. A $15 stock around the initial offering peaked around the $40 mark in 2018 as operating performance sputtered, and an M&A-focused strategy left the overleveraged business in trouble. In fact, shares fell to just a dollar in 2024 amidst weak demand post-pandemic, inventory destocking trends by customers, and supply chains opening up again.

Peak sales of around $8 billion had come down to some $5 billion in 2023, with EBITDA of $1 billion being quite solid, translating into solid margins. That was not the issue, but revenue declines and a near $9 billion net debt load created a massive debt overhang.

The company was thrown a financial lifeline in the summer of 2024 when Amphenol acquired the Outdoor Wireless Networks and Distributed Antenna Systems business in a $2.1 billion deal, but amidst continued revenue declines, the business was not out of the woods.

All this changed, as both firms announced a $10.5 billion deal for the connectivity and cable solutions last summer, the second deal in which Amphenol would buy assets of CommScope. With the sum of net debt and preferred equity position standing at $8 billion, that deal would translate into a pro forma net cash position of around $2 billion; that is after factoring in about half a billion in leakage costs. That net cash position would be worth about $9 per share based on 227 million shares outstanding.

Frankly, Amphenol's shareholders could ask if the company should not have tried to acquire CommScope entirely, after all, although Amphenol's track record gives few reasons to complain.

What Is left?

Outside the bag of money, it is interesting to look at RemainCo, which in the case of CommScope consists of Ruckus and ANS.

Ironically, both segments saw strong momentum as well last year. Ruckus posted a 46% increase in second-quarter sales to $190 million, with segment profits reported at $21 million. Revenues of ANS rose as much as 65% to $322 million for that quarter, with segment profits reported at $50 million.

Combined, these units post sales of around $2 billion each year, with operating profits tending to be around $300 million—that is, if we annualize the second quarter performance. Even if these activities were valued at 1 times sales and 7 times operating profits, a valuation of $2 billion would be equal to another $9 per share, for a sum-of-the-part valuation of $18 per share. Based on these hopes, shares rallied to the $14 mark when the deal was announced, gaining some 80% that day alone!

Shares kept gaining ground, having risen to $18 late in December, as the company made progress, including getting shareholder approval for the deal with Amphenol. Third-quarter RemainCo sales were reported at $516 million, that number comparing to $512 million in the second quarter. The issue is that segment profits were posted at $44 million, down from $70 million in the second quarter.

Besides a lower third earnings contribution from RemainCo, the issue is that more pressure was seen. This came after the company guided for full-year RemainCo EBITDA between $350-$375 million, which indicates a fourth-quarter EBITDA number seen between $70 and $75 million, down a bit from a $90 million number in the third quarter, as such setbacks can really impact the valuation of RemainCo.

Given the continued rerating while the RemainCo business was cooling off, I was looking for more clarity, awaiting the deal closing with Amphenol.

Making Inroads

On the 12th of January, the company announced the completion of the divestment of the Connectivity & Cable Solutions business to Amphenol. The company announced that it would pay off all debt and preferred equity and operate with a modest net debt load, sustainable by RemainCo. The special dividend is seen at a minimum of $10 per share, distributed in the coming 60-90 days.

The RemainCo business will be rebranded into the name Vistance Networks, as all this is largely in line with expectations. After all, net proceeds from the sale were pegged at $9 per share, as a $10 per share dividend would result in a very low leverage ratio, all being largely in line with expectations.

The other big news is that the Bloomberg report suggests that Extreme Networks (EXTR) is looking to acquire Ruckus Networks from RemainCo, now called Vistance. A deal could value this business at a billion, with Ruckus being the smaller business here. Based on the third-quarter performance, this business posts sales of around $700 million each year and $80 million in operating earnings, so a billion-dollar deal tag looks quite alright.

Pegging the net cash position pro forma the Amphenol sale at around $9 per share, a $1 billion deal for Ruckus could be worth about $4.50 per share, or more if there is upside to this number. This results in a $13.50 per share valuation, with shares trading at $19.50 per share here. The $6 per share discrepancy comes in at $1.36 billion in dollar terms, which would be attributable to ANS. This business has seen the strongest momentum recently, with the segment posting sales trending around $1.35 billion, with operating profits trending around a hundred million, both based on annualizing the third quarter results.

This implies that these assets are valued at around 1 times sales and about 13 times operating profits. All this looks largely fair here, as a fair amount of overhead and costs are incurred in all these (envisioned) transactions closing.

And Now?

At this point, shares look reasonably valued, although we could see changes given the implications of the Amphenol deal, a potential deal for Ruckus, and the performance of ANS. With shares having gained more ground, now trading near the $20 mark, and the sum-of-the-parts analysis becoming more clear, valuations look quite fair here.

As a result, I am not in a rush to chase the shares here but am keen to keep a close eye on this fascinating story, learning more about ANS, its potential, and handling of overhead as potentially more businesses get sold.

data.title: Diageo: Streamlining Through Divestments (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Diageo plc (DEO) is looking at noteworthy asset divestments. The spirit drink giant has already divested its East African operations, and is now reportedly looking critically at its weakly performing Chinese assets. Such non-core divestments aim to deleverage Diageo’s balance sheet and streamline operations towards high-margin, high-growth markets amid the company’s weak overall growth performance.

I maintained a Sell rating in my previous August 2025 article on the stock, titled “Diageo: More Than The Economy Behind Revenue Weakness.” The stock has since lost -14% of its value, losing to the S&P 500’s (SP500) 9% gain. The stock price now reflects justifiably weak growth expectations.

Diageo Is Streamlining the Business Through Divestments

Bloomberg recently reported that Diageo is evaluating strategic options for its Chinese assets. The company is reportedly considering an asset sale to local strategic buyers or private equity firms.

The strategic evaluation is focusing on especially Diageo’s 63% stake in Shanghai-listed Sichuan Swellfun that produces high- to mid-range baijiu drinks in the country. The majority stake is worth approximately $1.8 billion as of writing. Sichuan Swellfun’s financials are currently consolidated in Diageo’s financial statements. Diageo’s other Chinese assets that could be divested include the company’s own wholly owned subsidiary in China, including distilling and distribution of Diageo-owned brands in the country.

Diageo’s Chinese earnings have been very weak – the rationale for a divestment seems to be to position Diageo for better growth. Weak consumer confidence and CCP’s negative stance on drinking have led industry-wide alcohol sales to plummet in the country, causing Sichuan Swellfun to report a sharp decline in operating income in 2025 so far. Diageo showed a -9.0% organic sales decline in China in FY2025, and latest reported a “strong double-digit decline" in both volume and sales in Q1’FY2026. Market policies have made operations difficult in China.

Overall, the Chinese market is still relatively small for Diageo – the Asia Pacific region accounted for 18% of Diageo’s FY2025 net sales, and the Greater China region only made up approximately a quarter of the figure – while not reported accurately, around 4-5% of Diageo’s business is in China. The sale of the Sichuan Swellfun stake or other Chinese assets wouldn’t have a major impact on the investment case, albeit a divestment could streamline Diageo for slightly better earnings growth and enable the company to pay down debt.

Selling Chinese assets wouldn’t be Diageo’s first divestment – the company also recently agreed to sell its ownership in EABL to Asahi for $2.3 billion. The divestment includes Diageo’s beer business in East Africa as well as its ownership in UDVK, a Kenyan spirits producer. The East African business made up around half of Diageo’s operations in Africa and generated around 4-5% of Diageo’s total revenues similarly to China. East Africa showed minimal 0.1% revenue growth for Diageo in FY2025.

Diageo’s brands will continue to be sold in East Africa through a licensing agreement with Asahi. Diageo could also sell its wholly owned Chinese subsidiary with a similar structure.

Overall, asset disposals are within Diageo’s current strategy. Divestments aim to optimize Diageo’s long-term business footprint to its high-margin, high-growth assets instead of underperforming non-core ones. So far, such disposals have been based on geographical areas and not on non-core brands. Diageo’s interim CEO Manik Jhangiani noted in November that disposals also support the company’s plans to deleverage the balance sheet. Given Diageo’s nearly $24 billion in interest-bearing debts, prioritizing deleveraging seems justified.

Diageo’s Organic Growth Outlook Is Still Slow

Diageo has continued to report quite a slow topline performance. The company reported stagnant organic sales growth in Q1, reflecting a 2.9% increase in volume but a nearly similar decrease in price/mix.

By market, China caused the majority of Diageo’s weakness, leading to a -7.5% organic revenue decline in the Asia Pacific region. The performance remained weak in the U.S. as well, leading to a -2.7% organic revenue decline in the North America market. Very healthy growth in Europe, Latin America & the Caribbean, and Africa wasn’t enough to make Diageo’s total growth good.

The growth outlook remains weak past Q1 – Diageo already had to lower the FY2026 organic revenue guidance to “flat to slightly down” due to persisting weakness in China and the U.S. market.

Even if Diageo divests its Chinese operations, the long-term growth outlook is weak – increasing health awareness, GLP-1s, cannabis, and other factors have lowered younger people’s drinking in the U.S. market, accounting for a significant portion of Diageo's business. I believe that the trend is mostly secular, making Diageo’s growth outlook weak in North America even after FY2026.

According to Gallup’s survey, American adults’ drinking rate has gone down from 67% to 54% within only three years. The decline is the sharpest ever recorded in Gallup’s survey history, stemming largely from young adults' lower drinking. The trend bodes badly for Diageo, continuing to show in the U.S. performance.

Similar trend is carrying onto Europe as well. Diageo reported good 3.5% growth in Europe in Q1 due to Guinness’s successful current growth, but the overall market outlook remains weak. Without continuous market share gains, Diageo’s growth will be relatively slow in Europe, too.

Diageo still generates very healthy margins and even expects operating income to grow in the “low- to mid-single-digits” in FY2026. Margin gains are led by Diageo’s Accelerate program cost savings, stabilizing the mid-term earnings outlook.

DEO Stock’s Valuation Is Finally Fair

I updated my discounted cash flow (DCF) model for Diageo. I have factored the East Africa divestment into FY2026 assumptions, but don’t factor in any additional divestments until definitive agreements are announced.

Led by weak organic growth and the divestment in East Africa, I estimate revenues to decline by -5.5% in FY2026. Afterwards, I assume organic growth to stabilize at 1.5%, at a rate slightly below projected global inflation. The assumption reflects the industry’s weak long-term outlook.

I estimate the Accelerate program’s savings to contribute well towards margins, stabilizing the EBIT margin at 29.5%. Diageo’s high CapEx and current restructuring weaken the cash flow outlook, but I estimate Diageo to convert after-tax earnings relatively well to free cash flow over the long term.

The estimates put Diageo’s fair value at $92.0, near the stock price as of writing. The stock prices in weak growth in line with my expectations – I believe that Diageo’s risk-to-reward profile is now neutral after the stock has underperformed the overall market for quite a while.

The fair value estimate is up from $82.7 previously.

CAPM

The DCF model uses a cost of capital [WACC] of 7.02%, derived from a capital asset pricing model:

I again assume a 4% interest rate and a 30% long-term debt-to-equity ratio. For cost of equity, I use the 10-year bond yield of 4.16% as the risk-free rate. The equity risk premium of 4.18% is Professor Aswath Damodaran’s most recent January estimate for the U.S. I have kept the beta estimate at 0.59. With an ESG addon of 1.5% and a liquidity premium of 0.1%, the cost of equity stands at 8.23% and the WACC at 7.02%.

Takeaway

Diageo is looking at non-core divestments to streamline the business towards the best-performing markets and assets. The strategy allows Diageo to deleverage the balance sheet and try to improve growth. Secular trends are still pressuring the industry in the U.S. and Europe, though – the long-term growth outlook remains slow regardless of potential divestments in China and other weak markets.

Ultimately, DEO stock already prices in weak growth after a period of underperformance; I upgrade my rating to Hold.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Dell's Upside Is Mispriced Despite Memory Costs Headwinds
data.description:
Dell: Memory Upcycle Is Causing Some Concerns

The going hasn't been that straightforward for investors of Dell Technologies Inc. (DELL) over the past year. While the company is slated to be one of the leading contenders in the AI hardware/assembler space, the fact is being a hardware stock (no matter what they claim as competitive advantages due to inherent complexity) was going to be a tough ask to convince investors to revalue their multiples a lot higher than what we are seeing right now.

Well, the fact that DELL is still trading at a forward P/E of 10.4x, as compared to the tech median of 26x says a lot about the market's assessment, no? And who could really accord much blame for that "wild" disconnect, since margins bifurcation is a distinct concern in the current ramp, as the memory leaders have become front and center in this phase? I think it's notable that Dell has delivered a TTM free cash flow margin of under 3%, significantly below the sector median of 11.5%. With that, I don't think I need to impress further upon you that, unless Dell and its peers regain a firmer grip on managing the media narrative right now on the margins front, they are going to face an onerous task of seeing their multiples going up a lot higher from here. That said, one could also ask whether the level of pessimism right now in DELL stock has gone way too far below the sector median, while the company is still expected to stay profitable and resilient in the next couple of years.

Also, while Nvidia investors (NVDA) used to hog the limelight (and are still hogging the spotlight at the recent CES), the returns have been relatively underwhelming as compared to the rampaging run that seems like an apt descriptor for stocks like Micron (MU) or SK Hynix. In other words, the market's focus has shifted decisively to the storage/memory side of the AI value chain.

And that's going to be bad news for Dell and folks (or peers), because memory costs could further hurt their already thin margins. Thankfully, Dell does command a relatively strong business model in the enterprise space and commercial PC space that might be less susceptible to the more volatile consumer space. Hence, even as the spiking memory costs could impact the overall margins profile, Dell should be able to absorb these costs more resiliently.

It has also demonstrated that it's prepared to pull the necessary pricing levers to protect its margins, although the overall shipment estimates for the notebook PC segment are forecasted to decline further this year. In other words, it does seem like the year ahead for 2026 looks to be relatively challenged as the supply chain circumvents the immense challenges in this unprecedented memory upcycle. Therefore, could some sparkles and excitement be in store for Dell investors as the AI CapEx outlook for big tech and the leading hyperscalers is anticipated to surpass the $600B mark?

Dell's Margins Are Still Poised To Stay Resilient

Well, I guess the market is still biding its time, but they haven't totally given up on Dell yet, based on what I've assessed. In my previous Dell writeup, I based my optimism on the improved AI server momentum. Since then, Dell management has maintained its positive commentary and posture in this segment, which should help lift the ISG opportunity to greater heights in 2026 and beyond. Investors should note that Dell's AI backlog (reached $18.4B recently) has continued to grow. Notably, the cadence shows that the "pipeline continues to grow across Neocloud, sovereign and enterprise customers."

So I don't think there's a semblance of doubt that we are indeed looking at a multi-year ramp profile. That matters because if Dell can continue to scale quickly, it should help with improving the supply chain factors, and also helping to rein in the elevated costs, while also keeping Dell's demand factors robust. That is critical because it should mitigate the commoditized pressures alluded to the intense competition against its closest peers. Thus, as long as the battle royale doesn't turn into a "dog eat dog" scenario where these companies are compelled to engage in a destructive competition mainly on price due to tepid demand hangover, I believe we should observe more stable margins from more diversified players like Dell.

Wall Street also expects Dell to hold on to its FCF margins resolutely, trending nicely close to the 6.5% mark through FY2027. I believe that should augment the bullish narrative on Dell, particularly if enterprise AI adoption were to move into the ascendency. Its more firmly established credentials in the enterprise space beyond the usual hyperscaler customer base have arguably anchored it firmly in the next phase of the AI race.

I believe that while the hyperscalers and big tech have been leading from the front for the past few years, enterprise AI adoption has yet to truly catch up with the high optimism seen demonstrated by the gargantuan AI CapEx estimates. Hence, I believe that as long as investors are prepared to hang on tight to Dell's thesis, while navigating the near term constraints on the memory side of the ledger, the company could emerge strongly as the infra investment diffuses to the enterprise arena.

The question about timing will likely be debated. The question about whether the level of CapEx already committed will also be scrutinized. And the question about whether Dell could quickly convert its pipeline will need to be assessed. Nevertheless, management expressed its confidence that Dell is well-positioned to work with any AI chip partners, whether it's Nvidia, AMD (AMD), or even custom chips. Given the relatively low margins that define Dell's business model in AI as compared to its non-AI segments, the ability to scale quickly while keeping its costs well-contained will be front and center in this ramp.

Buy Or Sell DELL Stock?

For DELL stock, it's clear that the stock is consolidating above the $115 level. Attempts to shake out holders and force a further collapse haven't quite delivered the decisive blow for that bearish narrative to pan out. I've observed constructive dip-buying print over the past four months aligned to this level, suggesting that it's a key area of focus for the bulls/bears to do battle in early 2026 too.

Also, DELL's uptrend bias remains firm, showing that investors are still confident in its ability to capitalize on the AI themes. And with the structural AI themes helping to underpin further advances, I'm not about to make an about-face on the Dell story yet. I believe that the setup is leaning increasingly bullish, and when the market decides to look past the near-term memory costs hiccups and focuses on Dell's ability to deliver a sustainable ramp up over the next two years.

Rating: Maintain Buy.

data.title: Park Aerospace Has Everything I Want, Except The Right Price
data.description:
Thesis

This would be my fourth time writing about Park Aerospace Corp. (PKE). The company makes advanced composite materials used in aerospace, and its customers include major companies like GE and defense contractors. Last October, I kept a Hold rating on PKE. I noted the company’s balance sheet was unusually clean: no debt, and a lot of cash sitting there. However, I also warned that the stock price looked too high, profit margins were shrinking, factories were not being fully used, and the expected increase in demand from GE had not yet been proven.

Looking at performance, over the past one year the stock has jumped 66.45%, much better than the S&P 500 (SP500), which gained 19.32%; however, it didn't do as well as peer TAT Technologies (TATT), which rose 102.17%. It still outperformed Hexcel (HXL), which gained 25.51% over the same period.

After reviewing the latest earnings results to reevaluate the company’s fundamentals and update my opinion, I'm maintaining my neutral stance. There’s some real growth showing up in defense and aerospace. But the story doesn’t end there. Execution is still shaky, valuations are stretched, and those two facts alone are enough to keep a lid on near-term gains.

Park Aerospace: Operating in a Constrained Upswing

  • Sales, $17.333 million; gross profit, $5,903,000; Adjusted EBITDA, $4,228,000.

  • Sales estimate was $16.5 million to $17.5 million, so [Park Aerospace] came in within that range.

  • As of November 25, Airbus has already delivered 4,275 A320neo aircraft.
  • In December of '25, they delivered 97 airplanes.

Park Aerospace’s Q3 looked solid, even though the aerospace sector's still grappling with recovery pains. Demand is there, no question. But supply and execution issues haven’t gone away. Revenue didn’t move much, and that counts for something. That was mainly driven by GE Aerospace programs. In particular, the A320neo. Airbus is pushing deliveries higher, even as engine problems continue to slow things down.

  • Airbus is targeting a delivery rate of 75 per month.
  • The LEAP engine has a 64.5% market share.
  • At that delivery rate of 75 airplanes per month, that 64.5% market share translates into a lot of engines per 1,161.
  • As of September 30, '25, there were 7,900 firm LEAP-1A engine orders.

Management said that production of CFM’s LEAP engine has stabilized, which is helping Airbus move toward higher monthly aircraft production rates; but the broader supply chain is still a mess, pinched by geopolitics and missing materials, and it’s keeping any real production ramp-up on a short leash.

This lines up with what we’ve been seeing lately. Reuters says Airbus engines are still coming in very, very late. So, because of that, it makes sense that Park’s shipments slipped. International freight slowed things down, and customer-specific requirements didn’t help either. These problems are tied to competition in the market: Park benefits from being the only supplier of LEAP-1A parts, but customer certification processes are slowing how quickly those parts move through the system.

  • [Park Aerospace] had 0 sales of the fabric in Q3
  • [Park Aerospace] had a little bit more than $1 million of sales of the materials manufactured with C2B product in Q3.

On the defense side, the business mix improved. There were no low-profit C2B fabric sales, while shipments of ablative materials increased, which helped raise profitability. Basically, this mix shift comes from how OEMs stockpile inventory. Early on, they tend to hoard fabric for missile programs like Patriot. But over time, that fades, and you start seeing more higher-value processing instead.

  • Lockheed recorded increased production of Patriot PAC-3 interceptors by 60%.
  • Lockheeds Missile and Fire Control division received its biggest contract in history, $9.8 billion with a B award from the U.S. Army $9.8 billion with a B award from the U.S. Army.…for about 2,000 Patriot missiles.

The situation is becoming urgent. Ongoing wars in Europe and the Middle East have used up large amounts of military supplies, pushing the Pentagon to increase production as quickly as possible. On January 6, Lockheed Martin (LMT) announced a deal to triple production of PAC-3 missiles to 2,000 units per year by 2030; it basically backs up Park’s position as the sole North American distributor of Ariane’s fabric, at least in my view.

Even though management describes this as an "unprecedented" opportunity, there are still serious execution risks. Problems in the supply chain could lead to inconsistent deliveries, and Ariane’s current European production capacity may not align with U.S. demand unless the planned study is completed.

Operating Performance & Strategic Positioning

  • Gross margin, 34.1%.
  • Adjusted EBITDA margin, 24.4%.

Gross margins, meanwhile, widened. The product mix finally tilted the right way and production stayed balanced, which sidestepped the inventory drain that had chewed up earlier quarters. From an operations standpoint, performance remained stable. Even though the Kansas expansion increased costs in the short term, it helped set up the business to grow larger, which was important in this situation.

  • [Park Aerospace has] minimal impact on tariffs in [its] Q3.
  • [Park Aerospace prices] materials on a short-term basis.

I am still cautious about the positive outlook. Tariffs didn't have much effect during this period because the company adjusted its pricing to offset them, but rising trade tensions could change costs in the future if those higher costs cannot be fully passed on to customers.

  • 0 long-term debt.
  • $63.6 million of cash at the end of Q3.
  • Estimated capital budget for new plant, approximately $50 million.

The company has no debt and enough cash on hand to stay liquid, covering dividend payouts while also footing the bill for a $50 million plant meant to double production capacity. This approach is meant to help compete with large companies like GE and others in the defense sector. However, higher capital spending also brings new challenges, because working capital may fluctuate as inventory builds for ablative materials.

Total missed shipments in Q3, approximately 740,000.

Missed shipments increased sharply, showing that as the industry recovers and scales up again, supply chains are being strained which aligns with Deloitte’s outlook that volatility will continue through 2027. Park’s edge right now is that it can still bend when others can’t. It responds fast, it adjusts on the fly. But that advantage has an expiration date. Ongoing delivery delays could reduce this advantage as larger prime contractors invest more heavily in backup systems and redundancy.

  • Forecasted to include approximately $7.2 million of C2B fabric sales.
  • Forecast to include approximately $9.8 million of C2B fabric sales.
  • Park committed to contribute again… EUR 350,000 to the study.

Heavy C2B fabric releases are expected to boost sales in the fourth quarter, although margins may shrink as a result. This is reflected in the full-year outlook, where long-term attention to missile ramps is indicated by defense priorities like capacity studies and U.S. fabric talks.

PKE’s Valuation

Right now, PKE trades at about 48x trailing non-GAAP earnings. That’s well above its own five-year average of roughly 30. It’s also more than double the sector median, which sits near 22. This higher valuation's mainly due to short-term issues, including a sales mix that is heavily weighted toward lower-margin C2B fabric products, as well as uneven earnings caused by defense inventory stockpiling and General Electric production increases that are only beginning to show up in results.

They’re guiding to about $73 million in sales in fiscal 2026. At the same time, margins should expand as ablative materials take over. So, if that plays out, EPS could move up to roughly $0.52, versus the current trailing run rate of about $0.37. With a blended forward P/E ratio of about 46x, the company’s valuation begins to make sense given these long-term growth advantages. Of course, as long as it successfully delivers on its missile programs and maintains strong A320neo production volumes.

There are risks on both sides. The $50 million plant build could end up losing a lot of money if construction is delayed. There’s a possible risk of dilution if they tap the new ATM and sell shares below book. At the same time, the bigger prime brokers are stepping up automation, which makes some of this business feel redundant.

In the short term, EPS growth of 5.81% year over year is lower than other companies in the same sector. I realize it's a bit cliche, but because the stock is priced at a premium, the company must perform extremely well to justify that valuation.

Final Takeaway

Full disclosure, I personally took on a small position in PKE at the end of last year. Still, all things considered based on the data, I'm rating it a Hold. I think the setup skews to the upside. If missile production really ramps and GE volumes pick up, then capacity and sales could just straight up double. Existing holders, like myself, can stay long for the balance sheet strength and improving margins. I'd only buy more on a meaningful pullback. Momentum and value-focused investors should avoid at this price.

data.title: Crispr Therapeutics: Patience And Opportunity Through Volatility
data.description:
CRISPR Therapeutics (CRSP) continues to be one of the most puzzling stocks out there. Over the past year, the stock has swung wildly from a low of $31 to a high just north of $78, which is certainly much more volatile than the value of the underlying business. During a five-week period from the beginning of September to the first week in October, the stock exploded for what really seemed to be no specific reason, gaining 50% from the low 50s to the high 70s.

The distortion from its fundamental value is often jarring, and it almost doesn’t matter what you think that value is. As an investor, how do you react to these crazy price movements?

I believe you have to have a fundamental belief of what the value of the company is or could be and act accordingly. In CRISPR’s case, I believe that value is a lot higher than where it trades today. When the stock declines as it did earlier in 2025 and seems to do every few months, that’s the opportunity to be a buyer of the highest quality gene-editing company in the market.

If you’re bullish on CRISPR Therapeutics’ prospects as I am, you likely cheered the stock price run-up that we saw this fall but are now perplexed at the slow melt back into the mid-50s. Equally baffling was how this could have traded as low as it did back in April or May? CRISPR isn’t exactly a company that should be roiled by tariffs and global trade concerns, though once markets decide to move in one direction, very few companies are spared. This all came after another dizzying run-up in February 2025 that didn’t seem grounded in any company-specific news either. Has anything really transpired with the business that would justify any of these outcomes? In my view, the answer is unequivocally no.

CRISPR Therapeutics’ business moves slowly, glacially even. While significant progress continues to be made throughout its pipeline, the company only announces trial readouts once or twice a year. Moreover, it offers additional insight through presentations at conferences and quarterly earnings calls only a few more times a year. All the while, management and its researchers continue to work behind the scenes, making progress on potentially groundbreaking medical advances. Given how relatively young this company is compared to how long it takes new treatments to go through the FDA approval process and get to market, there’s not a whole lot that occurs day to day to justify substantial moves in this stock.

Let’s take a look at the key events that have occurred in the past several months for an indication of why the stock could be reacting how it is.

Q2 and Q3 Earnings Reports

There’s no better way to get a read on how a company is doing than reviewing its quarterly earnings report. Unfortunately, with a company like CRISPR, that’s just not true as very little of the value of the company has made its way to the P&L as of yet. In Q2, the earnings report article on Seeking Alpha stated:

As for the bottom line, CRSP’s GAAP net loss per share surged ~61% YoY to $2.40, exceeding the consensus by $1.01.

This is all true, but virtually meaningless at this point in the company’s history. In a normal quarter for a young company that currently does not have a sustainable source of revenue, earnings per share is not reflective of the business, particularly in a quarter that had an acquisition of in process R&D as part of a collaboration with Sirius Therapeutics, which caused havoc on its financials. The opposite can be true when one looks at a quarter in which CRISPR recorded a milestone payment for a collaboration with a large pharma company, such as its partnership with Vertex (VRTX). While this is a case of the company extracting value from its development, a one-time payment can’t be extrapolated or projected to determine the current value of the business. What is useful about their earnings releases? First, the updates to their development pipeline can be valuable information on the progress of the business, as discussed further below.

From a financial perspective, keeping an eye on the cash balance relative to the company’s free cash flows is important to ascertain that the company has the to breakeven or profitability, which is currently anticipated in 2028 according to CEO Samarth Kulkarni. It must have the cash to continue as a company until that point, without having to go to the capital markets which could be anti-dilutive to the stock. Clearly once the company begins to have earnings, it can then be valued like a normal business, based on the earnings or cash flows of the company.

The same thing can be said for the Q3 earnings report, in which CRISPR beat its EPS estimates by $0.07. This really holds no relevance to the long-term value of the company.

What does hold relevance to the long-term value of the company are the comments management makes on its progression with the many different assets that it continues to advance. CASGEVY is currently the one treatment that has made it past the finish line through FDA approval, but the ramp-up of patients is a slow process and one that is likely frustrating to many investors. While it has begun to record revenues, they currently appear as a net expense on its income statement, as currently, CRISPR’s share of expenses related to the rollout exceeds the gross revenues that it shares with Vertex. This will be an interesting line item to watch in future earnings releases, but to be clear, should not determine the financial outcome for the company over the long term, as there are many other assets that will contribute to that.

Collaboration with Sirius and Lilly

The company announced a collaboration with Sirius Therapeutics to co-develop and commercialize RNA-based treatments for thromboembolic disorders. This aligns with CRISPR’s CTX310 asset, currently going through phase 1 testing for heart disease indications. Interestingly enough, Sirius’ SRSD107 is also not a gene editing treatment, which has always been at the core of CRISPR Therapeutics pipeline, but rather a treatment that targets gene regulation. This subtlety seemingly opens the door for an array of different adjacent treatments that align with their current pipeline.

Additionally in December, CRISPR announced a supply agreement with Eli Lilly (NYSE: LLY) to enhance its CTX 112 asset, now known as zugo-cel, treating B-cell lymphomas. The agreement with Lilly gives CRISPR access to Lilly’s kinase inhibitor pirtobrutinib, which will be used to increase the potency and duration of the response to the malignancy. Whether this is successful or not, I think it’s great news for investors that CRISPR is partnering with the largest pharma company in the world, as CRISPR has many assets that will need resources to develop.

Other Noise Impacting the Stock

Sometimes it's not earnings or pipeline news that impacts stocks, it's news and information outside of the Company's control. Coverage was initiated at overweight by JP Morgan based on its pipeline potential and balance of portfolio. Notably, JPM stated:

we believe CRSP has the right strategy and sufficient reserves to avoid falling into the same trap that we see in some of its gene-editing peers by balancing its portfolio with an autoimmune cell therapy and an in vivo cardiovascular play.

This is significant as many of CRISPR’s competitors have not survived despite having assets with strong potential.

Takeover rumors are also a constant for CRISPR given its relatively low market cap compared to its potential. One would think this would act as a floor to the stock, but the experience of its peers confirms that there are no guarantees. I wouldn’t be buying CRISPR based on its potential to be acquired, and in fact, I’d argue that would be a bad outcome for investors, as I think any deal premium at these levels would be minuscule compared to the long-term potential of the company.

Valuation - The Hard Part

The valuation of CRISPR Therapeutics or any company in its situations continues to be a guessing game as no fundamental metrics underpin the stock. The stock volatility obviously has an impact on the future earnings or cash flows needed to justify the current valuation, so depending on when exactly you buy it determines how financially successful the company has to be. In other words, it’s the price you pay that matters.

The current market cap sits around $5.3 billion, which results in a $3.4 billion enterprise value after backing out the $1.9 billion of cash. Below are the current sector averages for valuation based on cash flows.

At the averages, that would indicate cash flow of $225 million a year to justify a sector average multiple, though sector average is very conservative for a company just beginning revenue generation. While currently, the trailing twelve-month operating cash flows are a negative $303 million, driven by $488 million loss in that same time frame, management is expecting profitability in 2028 which should improve cash flow significantly.

Whether you believe this is possible or not, a company like CRISPR is extremely difficult to value given a lack of fundamentals.

Major Risks to the Thesis

The major risks around CRISPR involve the success of its current FDA approved treatment Casgevy, the continued development of its pipeline, and its ability to withstand the next two years of losses and cash outflows before the company hopefully breaks even. These risks are just weakened versions of the risks that have existed ever since CRISPR was a public company. Casgevy continues to be adopted, but the process remains long, expensive, and very difficult for the patient. That said, initial reports indicate that quality of life is improved significantly following treatment, which is positive for those with severe Sickle Cell disease. Whether the expense and difficulty of treatment is worth it for those with milder symptoms remains to be seen and thus the commercial success of Casgevy will not be known for some time.

The commercial success of Casgevy is just a subset of the main risk, whether the company can stand on its own financially in time to get its many pipeline assets to market. Many other competitors in this space failed this test, but CRISPR has always been stronger financially and now that it’s 2026, the company is not that far away from generating real earnings and cash flows.

Conclusion

Bottom line: Charlie Munger once said that “the big money is not in the buying and selling, but in the waiting.” Unfortunately, Tom Petty also said that “the waiting is the hardest part,” but that is my advice to you.

As someone who has followed this stock closely since owning it beginning in 2022, I believe the stock is manic, and if I were a lot smarter with trading, I could have made a lot of money over the years selling when the stock is in the $60s or $70s and buying it when it is around $40. That said, I will not sell when this stock reaches the $70s once again, as I remain convinced that someday, maybe in the next six months or maybe not for six years, this stock will keep going through $80 to the $500 a share I think it can eventually attain.

So far that hasn’t happened, but someday I think it will, and I’m going to wait around until it does. But don’t be hesitant to pick up some shares should the bottom fall out once again and the stock return to the low $40s range.

data.title: Eikon Therapeutics Launches IPO For Ambitious Oncology Pipeline Development
data.description:
A Quick Take On Eikon Therapeutics' IPO

Eikon Therapeutics, Inc. (EIKN) has filed to raise development capital in an IPO of its common stock, according to SEC S-1 registration information.

The company is a large biopharma firm developing drug treatment candidates for various cancer conditions.

It has show positive initial trial results for its lead program, is well capitalized, backed by well-regarded institutional venture capital firms and has a top tier leadership team.

I’ll provide a final opinion when we get more details about the IPO’s pricing and valuation assumptions.

Eikon's Overview And Development Efforts

Millbrae, California-based Eikon Therapeutics, Inc. was founded to develop a range of treatments for immuno-responsive cancer conditions.

Management is led by Chair and Chief Executive Officer Roger M. Perlmutter, M.D., Ph.D., who has been with the firm since 2021 and was previously EVP of Merck (MRK) and President of Merck Research Laboratories and was previously EVP and Head of Research and Development at Amgen.

The firm's lead candidate, EIK1001, is in Phase 2 trials for the treatment of melanoma and non-small cell lung cancer (NSCLC).

Management expects the first interim analysis for its melanoma trial in the second half of 2026 and the Phase 2 readout for its NSCLC trial in Q4 2026.

The current status of the firm’s development pipeline is shown here:

Eikon has booked fair market value investment of $1.2 billion through September 30, 2025, from investors, including Lux Capital, Foresite Capital, The Column Group, Innovation Endeavors, Mahler International Limited and Catalyst4.

Eikon’s Markets & Competitors

According to a 2025 market research report by Grand View Research, the global market for melanoma drug treatments was an estimated $5.8 billion in 2024 and is forecasted to exceed $10.2 billion by 2030.

This represents a forecast CAGR of 9.9% from 2025 to 2030

The primary factors driving this expected growth are an aging population, rising incidence of skin cancers and growing awareness and treatment options for patients.

Also, the chart below shows the historical and projected future growth trajectory of the global melanoma treatments market by region, through 2030:

Major competitive companies that have developed or are developing similar treatments include the following firms:

  • Bristol-Myers Squibb Company (BMY)

  • AstraZeneca (AZN)

  • Novartis AG (NVS)

  • Merck & Co. (MRK)

  • Amgen (AMGN)

  • F. Hoffman-La Roche Ltd. (RHHBY)

  • Abbott Laboratories (ABT)

  • Others.

The company is actively developing treatment candidates for other, large market size areas of cancer conditions.

Eikon's Financial Results

The company’s recent financial results are typical of a development-stage biopharma, since they feature no revenue and significant R&D and G&A expenses associated with its ambitious pipeline of programs.

Here are the firm’s current financial results for the periods indicated, per its recent IPO filing documents:

As of September 30, 2025, the company had $281 million in cash, equivalents and marketable securities and $292 million in total liabilities, including $237 million in operating lease liabilities.

Eikon's IPO Details

Eikon has filed sell shares of its common stock in an IPO that could reach $400 million.

No existing shareholders have shown an interest in acquiring shares at the IPO price, at least as detailed in the first IPO prospectus.

Company, shareholder and executive lock-ups will be for the usual 180 days after the effective date of the prospectus.

Management said it plans to use the IPO net proceeds as detailed here:

to advance the clinical development of EIK1001, our most advanced product candidate...

to advance the clinical development of EIK1003...and EIK1004

to advance the clinical development of EIK1005... and the preclinical and potential clinical development of EIK1006

to fund additional discovery and preclinical activities for existing and future programs, our technology platform, and enabling capabilities; and

the remainder for general corporate purposes, working capital, and other capital expenditures.

(Source: SEC.)

Leadership’s online company roadshow presentation is not yet available.

Pertaining to legal proceedings, management said the company is not currently subject to any legal actions that would have a material adverse effect on its business.

Listed bookrunners of the IPO are J.P. Morgan, Morgan Stanley, BofA Securities and Cantor.

My Thoughts About Eikon’s IPO

EIKN is seeking US public market capital to advance its substantial pipeline through and into the trials process.

The firm’s lead candidate, EIK1001, is currently in Phase 2 trials for treating melanoma and non-small cell lung cancer.

Leadership says the first interim analysis for its melanoma trial should occur in the second half of 2026 and the initial readout of its Phase 2 trial for its NSCLC candidate should be in Q4 2026, both relatively near-term milestones.

The company's strategy is primarily focused on developing internally-derived therapies, although it will consider in-licensing promising technologies, which it did with its current lead program, EIK1001.

Trial results for EIK1001 have been positive, with no dosing limitations, a small number of adverse events and an overall response rate of 60% in its initial Phase 2 trial.

The market opportunity for treating the various cancers the company is producing candidates for is extremely large, with the solid tumor market a multi-hundred billion dollar market alone.

Management disclosed pharma firm collaboration relationship with Seven and Eight Biotherapeutics Corp., Impact Therapeutics (Shanghai), Superb Wisdom Limited, and MSD International Business GmbH.

The company’s investor syndicate includes a number of highly regarded institutional venture capital firms such as Lux Capital and Foresite Capital.

Eikon’s senior management team is also extremely qualified, with its CEO a top tier executive in major pharmaceutical firm ranks.

Risks for investors include management’s designation of the company as a "smaller reporting company" and "emerging growth company," which enables the firm to provide substantially less information for shareholders.

Many of these types of company stocks have performed poorly post-IPO, although they have typically been in non-life-science industries.

The company is highly capitalized, having raised substantial venture capital investment, and the IPO proceeds will further capitalize it as it moves more of its development programs into and beyond the Phase 1 stage of trials.

By biopharma firm standards, Eikon’s IPO will likely see significant institutional investor demand, especially in a generally life science IPO-constrained market environment that features positive post-IPO results for several biopharma IPOs in recent months.

I’ll provide a final opinion when we learn management’s pricing and valuation assumptions for the IPO.

Expected IPO Pricing Date: To be announced.

data.title: JPMorgan Chase: The Economic Conundrum
data.description:
JPMorgan Chase (JPM) reported earnings yesterday. But the real deal, as has been noted in the last article and articles before that, is the economic situation. The last article noted high asset values and the political uncertainty. This and a possible recession have the bank remaining vigilant, as noted in the latest earnings release. The problem is that the economy continues to do well, and this has got more than a few people thinking that the threats to the economy are disappearing. But economic research does not have a time frame attached to any conclusion. The reason is that any conclusion is dependent upon what several relevant organizations do combined with “changing our mind,” as we constantly seem to do. The whole situation put together means that results may take a while to become visible to the average person.

The bank noted that in the latest quarter, the consumer continues to spend. So far, that has not resulted in more charge-offs for this bank.

But those of us who remember past incidents like the early 1970s know that sometimes economics takes longer than people expect to play out. In the case of the 1970s, the warnings went on for at least a year, and many thought the warnings were just plain crazy or irrelevant towards the end. But in the end, the 1970s proved to be extremely challenging for all of us.

That does not necessarily mean that this time around will be an exact replay of what happened then. Economics is uncertain enough that timing can prove to be quite a challenge. However, what appears to be a problem is the dual actions of an increasing deficit combined with a call for lower interest rates. I still have my Samuelson textbook, “Economics.” The conclusion that rising deficits combined with low interest rates is asking for trouble has not changed in all the years I have had that textbook.

But like any other situation, times are currently good, and a change in strategy can mitigate whatever might happen or even completely offset it. As a country, we love to borrow money. But we hate to repay it. Again, as a country, we have long denied the effects of debt on our budget because good times hide the budget effects.

Right now, we are focused on high prices without really realizing that the time to deal with those high prices is now, before another round of inflation drives them higher still (potentially). Instead, we as a country blame whoever is in office at the time of inflation without realizing it can take years to build up enough pressure for that inflation to appear. It generally does not happen overnight (or even in the 12 months before that).

Earnings

It is in this current atmosphere that the bank reported earnings. The vigilance referred to not only concerns future banking strategies but also concerns the possibility that the loan loss reserves in a number of categories could grow. That may mean that bank earnings are at a cyclical high.

The economic conundrum that overhangs earnings is that “everything is going great guns,” but as noted before, there are plenty of warning signs that the future is just loaded with challenges (to say the least). At least with a money center bank like this one, there is a lot of diversification. That diversification should allow for an outperformance compared to much of the industry for whatever the future holds.

As noted before, the business is doing just fine. The provision for credit losses was due to the forward purchase of the Apple credit card business. Otherwise, the quarterly earnings comparison would have shown some growth over the previous fiscal year.

As it was, the annual growth in earnings was still positive.

For the time being, there is guidance (a budget of sorts) on the goals for fiscal year 2026. However, economic events could play a part in how that budget actually unfolds as the fiscal year develops. My sense is that there is a bit more uncertainty going forward than is usually the case. That could affect the stock price.

Banking in general is all about matching revenues to expenses. But the higher than usual uncertainty is about making sure that the assumptions for that matching still hold. The uncertainty could make this bank and others head towards a lot of short-term matching at the expense of longer-term deals (like loans) in the name of safety. It is not that long ago that I was covering banks where the perceived matching came undone, resulting in a bank run and, of course, a bank takeover (or other arrangements, as a few quickly merged).

Banks, in particular, hate uncertainty because public trust is such a big part of the business. So right now, we appear to be about to enter a period of higher uncertainty where investing in larger banks is a good strategy until this period passes.

Summary

JPMorgan Chase remains a strong buy consideration. It is one of the most profitable money center banks that I follow. Money center banks have the advantage of considerable geographic diversification as well as lines of business diversification. Their sheer size and diversification often allow them to outperform challenging banking periods compared to much of the industry.

This often makes an idea like this suitable to a wide variety of investors. Furthermore, the CEO, Jamie Dimon, has been around long enough that whatever he says publicly and in interviews carries an outsized weighting with many, including the Federal Reserve. In short, listening to this CEO is a very good way to understand the economic situation.

I would consider buying and holding this for as long as Jamie Dimon is CEO. Under his leadership, this bank has long outperformed other money center banks. That is no small task given the size of this banking operation.

Risk

The biggest risk is that the current economy is doing well despite the increasing deficit and the lowering of interest rates. Probably what should happen under classic economics is higher interest rates to help offset the potential inflationary (future) impact of increasing deficits. But no one likes higher interest rates at the current time, just as no one sees the need for higher interest rates because inflation is currently low.

Similarly, the increasing budget deficits have to be financed. The United States debt rating has been lowered for the first time that anyone can remember. That should be a reminder that borrowing now gives us less financial flexibility in the future (as a country).

All of this combined means that this bank and, likely, the whole industry are doing just fine now. However, the banking industry sees potential challenges or outright trouble ahead that voters really do not want to do anything about (as a group). Until that banking view changes, meaning banks see politicians take action to lessen future challenges, banks will continue to lend cautiously, which could affect economic activity. The more conservative banks become, the less lending will happen. That can make the job of the Federal Reserve all that more challenging.

For the time being, banking results are fine, and the market appears to be ok with the banking results posted so far. When that view or if that view will change is anyone's guess. For now, warnings abound, but they do not appear to be taken seriously by anyone (in a material position) outside of banks.

data.title: 3D Systems: Cash Flow Breakeven Could Unlock Multiple Expansion
data.description:
Founded in 1986 by the inventor of 3D printing himself, Chuck Hull, 3D Systems (DDD) is the world’s first mover in the 3D printing business. The company develops 3D printers to help businesses in certain industries, such as healthcare, to print intricate parts generally harder to do by humans in a precise way, such as orthopedic implants or dental aligners.

Since its IPO in 1988, all-time performance has been volatile and largely underwhelming, marked by an all-time high of $96 per share in around 2014 and deep correction over the next few years. However, DDD has recently experienced a 35% surge YTD, though the stock is still down by around -20% on a 1-year basis.

I initiate my coverage with a buy rating. My 1-year price target of $2.9 presents a 17% upside from the current price of $2.47 per share.

Financial Reviews

Overall, fundamentals have been relatively weak. DDD’s revenue, for instance, has been consistently declining over the past five years.

In Q3, revenue was down -19.2% to $91.2 million, missing the estimates. While the divestment of Geomagic could have negatively affected revenue decline, the management also highlighted that the weakness in customer CAPEX spending was the main driver for Q3’s underperformance, suggesting that it could be something more structural on the demand side, in my view. Nonetheless, the positive note here is that the Aerospace & Defense (A&D) segment grew 50% YoY, suggesting that there is a decent potential in this niche going forward.

Meanwhile, DDD continued to be net unprofitable for almost the past five years as well from GAAP standpoint, despite consistently delivering over 30% gross margin. Nonetheless, GAAP net loss narrowed in Q3 to $18 million, as did adjusted EBITDA loss, which narrowed to $10.8 million. The main drivers for these improvements were the aggressive cost saving initiative throughout 2025. In my opinion, though profitability has improved, the pressure on gross margin has been quite apparent as a result of product mix shift towards lower-margin hardware as opposed to higher-margin software offerings due to Geomagic sales.

DDD’s liquidity and cash flow generation were equally challenging as of Q3, which was also the reason why the company also took a bold cost-saving action, in my view. Ending Q3 with only around $95.7 million of cash and short-term investments, my analysis suggests that DDD may only have approximately just a little bit more than a year of runway, considering the average $25 million of net cash burn per quarter. At least since four years ago, DDD’s main source of liquidity has never been operating cash flow (OCF), which was -$73 million the past nine months ending in Q3. Within the same period, though, $108.3 million of investing cash flow provided a breath of fresh air for DDD. A lot of that was the proceeds from the sale of Geomagic.

Catalyst

In my opinion, catalysts remain minimal, with the narrative around any positive re-rating in 2026 should revolve around DDD’s ability to reach positive profitability and OCF, ideally from the continuation of cost-saving to drive operating expenses (OpEx) down, instead of an aggressive execution on its growth story. As per the management’s comment in the earnings call, DDD is on track to reach its $50 million of annualized savings target by the end of the year:

Looking ahead, we expect continued reductions in expenses through the end of the year and are targeting fourth quarter operating expenses to be marginally below the current quarter. To date, we are on track to deliver over $50 million in annualized savings by year-end.

Source: Q3 earnings call.

On the back of that, my view is that if DDD could use that momentum to reach positive OCF or at least breakeven in the first half of 2026, there is a chance for positive re-rating for the stock. Given the 4-year history of negative OCFs, a breakeven this year would suggest that DDD is out of the woods in terms of survival mode, and is ready to take the first step in growing its business with minimal fear of further dilutive capital raise or bankruptcy.

On that note, management’s guidance for an 8% to 10% sequential growth in Q4 suggests that DDD is in a good position to capture the momentum in 2026 to bring it closer to its next growth story. Also, At such a rate, DDD will experience the strongest sequential QoQ growth after a period of sequential decline over the past year.

I believe the growth story of DDD will hinge on the recent development in the A&D sector that is in favor of the 3D printing industry. In alignment with DDD’s management’s view about A&D, an article by 3D Printing Industry publication suggests that the recent government initiative has fueled A&D demand for domestic 3D printing systems:

U.S.-based 3D printer manufacturer 3D Systems has reported notable developments in its Aerospace & Defense (A&D) segment, driven by revenue growth projections, a major U.S. facility expansion, and technological advancements. These initiatives aim to meet rising demand for secure domestic manufacturing in national security and space programs, a trend supported by the Fiscal Year 2026 National Defense Authorization Act (NDAA), which restricts foreign-sourced 3D printing systems for Department of Defense (DoD) programs.

Source: 3dprintingindustry.com.

Risk

I think that risks remain moderate to high. While the result of the cost-saving initiative is a promising step towards turnaround, I believe that DDD’s weak liquidity and cash generation today, which will continue to pressure DDD to seek outside financing, may limit the potential of valuation re-rating.

As I mentioned earlier in the previous section, the recent $31 million equitization transaction just last month resulted in the exchange of convertible notes to 16.6 million new shares, driving more dilution. In my view, if DDD fails to reach positive OCF in 2026, we should expect further dilutive capital raise.

Furthermore, all of that could be worsened if macro and tariff uncertainties continue to affect spending, which the management flagged as a demand-side issue in Q3:

As has been the case over the last several quarters, this overall softness continues to be driven by our customers' muted CapEx spending for new production capacity stemming from uncertainty around tariffs. As such, we've taken aggressive actions to adjust our cost structure while maintaining core R&D investments to position the company for long-term growth when market conditions improve.

Source: Q3 earnings call.

Valuation/Pricing

My target price for DDD is driven by the following assumptions for the bull vs bear scenarios of the FY 2026 projection, based on consensus estimate:

  1. Bull scenario (50% probability) assumptions - DDD reaches OCF breakeven, and A&D segment grows by 20% to 50% YoY, offsetting the loss of revenue from Geomagic, eliminating the need to do further capital raise. After the $31 million of equitization conversion in December 2025, shares outstanding was approximately 145 million. I expect P/S to expand to 1.5x, slightly higher than DDD’s 1-year P/S peak.

  2. Bear scenario (50% probability) assumptions - DDD fails to reach OCF positive and need to raise another round of dilutive financing, raising the number of shares to 155 million while A&D growth stalls as a result of contract delays with dental market softness persisting. P/S to drop to 0.7x, lower than where it is today.

Consolidating all the information above into my model, I arrived at an FY 2026 weighted target price of $2.9 per share, presenting a potential upside of 17% from the current level. At this point, I would give the stock a buy rating.

Conclusion

While DDD has done well in terms of finding outside financing to continue sustaining its business, 2026 will be the time when it could use the proceeds to capture some of the opportunities in A&D to deliver meaningful growth. However, in my opinion, the first priority will remain reaching the cost saving target. I think that my analysis suggests that DDD is attractively priced. In my opinion, it is a high-risk opportunity, but less speculative in nature. The macro catalyst continues to support the growth thesis, while the continuation of cost saving initiative should drive the OCF breakeven thesis. As it stands, those two are the keys to unlock positive share re-rating.

data.title: Groupon: This Marketplace Could Be At An Inflection Point (Upgrage)
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Earlier last year I wrote a bearish article on Groupon (GRPN). My main criticism of Groupon is that fundamentally the business model is flawed. For a two-sided marketplace to succeed in economic terms, both parties, merchants and consumers, must find it beneficial to use the platform. Showing little signs of improvement, reflected by double-digit growth in billing and positive free cash flow, investors turned bullish.

When my article got published, Groupon's share price was at $18; after the publication, the stock rose to almost $38, a 106% price appreciation in just 2 months. Since then the stock has corrected and currently stands at 16%, an 11% price drop since my write-up. The main takeaway was that even though a company could have weak fundamentals amid a broken business model, the price could easily move positively the minute the sentiment from investors changes.

YoY, Groupon returned 35.61%, outperforming the S&P 500 and Etsy (ETSY), which both returned 20.97% and 17.71%, respectively. And slightly underperformed eBay (EBAY) with roughly 5 percentage points. Looking at Q3 earnings results, Groupon has rolled off old convertible debt with a new one at a higher cost of capital, increasing their interest expenses. Amid the resolution of tax disputes with the Italian authorities, both events are estimated to reduce 2026 FCF (the tax dispute settlement alone is estimated to reduce FCF by $15 million).

In spite of these headwinds, revenue growth, operating margins, OCF, and FCF have flipped to being positive. The fundamentals on this name are starting to inflect, forcing me to rethink my bearish thesis; as a result, I am upgrading Groupon to a hold.

What Bulls are saying

To be less biased on names I was bearish on, I find it to be a healthy exercise to read bullish takes. One bullish thesis came from the fellow analyst Tunga Capital, stating the firm is working on improving merchant economics. One way the company is doing this is by improving its matching algorithm so that discount deals from merchants go to targeted consumers and by enforcing repeat purchases through WOW deals. Also, management stated efforts to improve the user experience of the mobile app.

I believe these efforts are truly a step in the right direction for the company; however, how and when these efforts will reflect positively in financial terms on a sustainable basis is yet to be known, but recently we are starting to see earning margins turning positive, which could imply improvement in merchant economics.

Financial Analysis

Q3 2025 Income Statement

When looking at the earnings results of the latest quarter, we see that Groupon grew North America revenue 10% YoY to $96.01 million, while the international market shrunk 3% to $26.82 million. In aggregate, this resulted in total revenue increasing 7% to $122.83 million, beating consensus estimates by $839,000.00. Cost of revenue declined 5% to $10.99 million, resulting in gross profits increasing 9% to $111.84 million and landing gross margins at 91%. Operating expenses increased 1% to $109.64 million. The growth was driven by marketing spend increasing by 14% and offset by a 6% decline from SG&A. Operating income for the quarter was positive, as witnessed by a 139% increase compared to the prior year quarter to the value of $2.19 million, landing EBIT margins at 1.78%. Other expenses increased drastically by more than 500% YoY to $98.72 million, and the main reason for such an increase was due to the accounting loss north of $100 million the firm realized from the extinguishment of debt; this resulted in the pretax loss of $6.53 million.

Adjusted EBITDA for the quarter was $18 million, and GAAP net loss was $118 million. Normalized EPS was -$0.21, and GAAP EPS was -$2.92, missing consensus estimates by $0.46 and $2.95, respectively. For the fourth quarter, the consensus is estimating revenues of $137.34 million (implying a 5% YoY growth) and positive GAAP EPS of $0.16, a substantial difference from an EPS of -$1.20 that was realized in Q4 2024. These estimates show analysts' expectation of improved fundamentals.

9-Month Income Statement

Looking at the 9-month duration period, we can see that revenue growth was almost flat (rising by just 1%) at $365.72 million, bolstered by a 3% growth from North America and offset by a 5% decline from international markets. Cost of revenue declined 8% to $33.15 million, resulting in gross profits increasing 2% to $332.56 million. Operating expenses declined 1% to $315.43 million; the decline was mainly driven by SG&A spending shrinking 9% to $198.15 million while marketing increased 15% to $117.28 million, leading to operating income increasing 179% to $17.13 million and landing operating margins at 4.68%, which is a substantially higher margin compared to Q3, illustrating that on a trailing basis, operating margins are expanding.

9-Month Cash Flow Statement

The results from the cash flow statement are the ones I was most looking forward to, as management stated improving free cash flow as one of its priority goals alongside adjusted EBITDA. On non-cash items we see a net $92.58 million cash add-back, highly influenced by a $99 million account loss that was recorded on the income statement, which resulted in the company recording a net loss of $89.16 million for the 9-month duration. Operating cash flow, however, increased 510% YoY to $7.89 million, offset by a cash outflow of $37.44 million from working capital needs. With CAPEX at $11.05 million, free cash flow was negative at $3.16 million.

Groupon fundamentals on a trailing basis show some improvements; revenue declines have stabilized to moderate growth, operating margins have flipped to being positive since 2024, and the same trend is true with operating and free cash flow.

One chart that really convinced me to rerate Groupon to a hold is the revenue growth chart on a quarterly basis. We can see that during the 2020-2023 period Groupon was experiencing massive revenue declines; during 2024 the decline rate decreased to single digits, and as of the last quarter, it flipped positive to 7%, a record growth since 2016. It's still not clear whether this positive growth rate will be sustained going into 2026; however, this shows significant improvements in the company's fundamentals.

Q3 Balance Sheet

Despite the massive cash balance of $238.5 million, the state of Groupon's balance sheet raises few concerns for me, especially now that total liabilities have just surpassed total assets by $59.4 million.

This sudden increase in liabilities was driven by the firm's action of rolling over old convertible debt with new one for a premium north of $75 million. This action was to extend debt maturities from 2026 and 2027 to 2030. Compared to old notes that totalled $170 million with a weighted coupon rate of 5.647%, these new notes are worth a principal value of $244 million (43% higher than the old principal value) and have a coupon rate of 4.875%. Totalling everything, this new debt has a higher cost of capital than the old one, which will increase Groupon's interest expenses and negatively affect the bottom line. The chart below shows that coming into 2025, Groupon maturities were nearing, and the company, doubting it might have enough liquidity to cover these maturities, was forced to issue new notes at a higher premium. Now the debt balance has increased to $343.6 million, with the current portion worth $33.6 million.

Valuation

Compared to peers, Groupon has always traded at a discount, which is not surprising to me given my previous stance on the firm's business model. During the mid-year 2025 stock rally, the company for the short run reached the EV/revenue multiple of Etsy, but the correction contracted the multiple back to near its 3-year rolling median. Historically we can see that the company still trades at a slight premium to its rolling median, with the median at 1.05 vs. its current multiple of 1.5. I think this is a fair valuation, and until the company improves the merchant economics, the discounted valuation to peers is still warranted.

Risk

Even though I'm upgrading my rating from a sell to a hold, we still don't have enough running data to confidently state that Groupon's fundamentals are at an inflection point. And given the $25 million settlement with the Italian tax authorities, I expect the bottom line and FCF to be pressured, which could lead to a further price decline.

Conclusion

Groupon is definitely an interesting company to write about. I believe the firm currently has a flawed business model, but recent fundamental improvements tell me the company might be working on improving merchant economics, which could be a substantial change if this goal gets achieved. What makes a two-sided marketplace thrive is its ability to offer a compelling value proposition to both the supply and the demand side. Historically, Groupon hasn't done a good job in serving the supply side, but recent improvements in revenue growth and operating margins suggest the company is doing something different to help out merchants. It's still too early to turn bullish; hence, I remain neutral until more evidence is presented.

data.title: Salesforce: Cheap 14x FCF Multiple And Agentforce Acceleration
data.description:
A few years ago, it would have been unthinkable to imagine that Salesforce (CRM), widely considered the original cloud software company, would trade at discounted multiples of earnings. Salesforce has long been known to be a growth-at-all-costs company, prioritizing product development and even splashy M&A deals to grow as large as possible.

Today, its operating mantra has flipped. While large-cap tech rivals like Microsoft (MSFT) and Meta (META) are hiring heavily and boosting capex to compete in the AI arms race, Salesforce has turned its focus to profitability. It's that more conservative operating nature that I think gives Salesforce a lot of credibility if the S&P 500 undergoes a bearish cycle this year, and why I'm keen to buy the stock after a ~25% slide over the past year.

I last wrote a "Buy" article on Salesforce in October, when the stock was trading slightly below $250 per share. Since then, Salesforce has delivered a beat-and-raise quarter in Q3, while also lifting its free cash flow expectations for the year. I continue to underline that Salesforce is now a true value stock, especially in the large-cap tech space where stretched multiples abound. Amid acceleration as well in the company's AI-driven Agentforce product, I'm reiterating my "Buy" rating on this company.

As a reminder for investors who are newer to this stock, here is my full long-term bull case on Salesforce:

  • Aggressive growth targets through FY30. Though from a stock market perspective Salesforce appears to have been left behind in the dust, the company has initiated very aggressive growth targets through FY30 (calendar 2029). The company aims to generate at least $60+ billion in revenue in that year, which would imply a ~10% growth CAGR from now until FY30. Given Salesforce usually has a track record for guiding conservatively, it's likely that the company will continue to nudge this goal upward. The fact that RPO (essentially backlog) is already growing at a low teens pace helps to provide support behind these targets.
  • Agentforce is a material growth driver. ARR across the company's "Agentforce" line of agentic AI-driven products is more than doubling y/y. Unlike many other software companies for whom AI is merely a buzzword without material growth to show for it, Salesforce is making a dent with its AI products - even if it isn't as loud, or as flashy with its spending as some of its other larger-cap peers.
  • Broad product portfolio to cross-sell with. In a time of budget tightness for many companies, many IT departments have implemented more vendor concentration: reducing the number of "point solution" tools that aim to address only a single issue. Salesforce has a huge product portfolio across sales, marketing, internal communications, APIs - essentially anything that's not ERP or backend infrastructure related, Salesforce has. This creates a powerful flywheel of products to cross-sell into customers and grow its wallet share.
  • Rule of 40. Salesforce is achieving ~10% revenue growth amid a mid-30s pro forma operating margin, positioning the stock easily within the Rule of 40 that many software companies aspire to.

There's an overwhelming narrative in the stock market that AI is out to disrupt the cloud software industry, which were themselves the disruptors of IT merely a few years back. To me, these fears are unfounded: while players like Claude's Anthropic are certainly making a dent in enterprise AI, there's no evidence yet that companies can rip out specialized applications like Salesforce's Sales Cloud in favor of a new enterprise AI app that can run those functions.

Take advantage of the ~20% slide over the past year as a buying opportunity.

Q3 download

Let's now go through Salesforce's latest quarterly results in greater detail. The Q3 earnings summary is shown below:

Salesforce's revenue grew 8.6% y/y to $10.26 billion, keeping virtually the same pace of growth as in Q2. The chart below, meanwhile, showcases the company's growth rates by product line:

Where we are impressed is the company's results in Sales (originally named Sales Cloud). Across the enterprise software sector, sales and marketing-related software products have seen compression in growth rates thanks to customers de-prioritizing growth (in other words, sales hiring is more challenging in today's environment). This has put pressure on seat counts. And yet, though Salesforce's core Sales product (which it's most famous for and is the industry standard CRM) is no longer exhibiting startup-like growth rates at its scale.
