We do note that Salesforce's marketing products are experiencing a bit more pressure, with growth decelerating to 1% year/y.

That said, this is more than offset by acceleration in Agentforce/platform revenue, which grew 19% y/y and has achieved four sequential quarters of acceleration. Meanwhile, the snapshot below showcases the company's direct ARR from gen AI and Agentforce products, which hit $540 million in Q3 and grew at a stunning 330% y/y pace. Again, AI isn't just a marketing ploy for Salesforce: it's actually generating superb revenue growth from its agentic AI products.

Another core indicator of strong growth is RPO, which is effectively backlog. Total RPO grew 12% y/y, while current RPO (which is the portion of backlog that will be recognized as revenue within the next 12 months) grew 11% y/y. Both of these are ahead of current revenue growth, and ahead of the company's implied 10% growth CAGR target through FY30 (specifically, getting from $41.50 billion in this year's FY26 guidance midpoint to $60 billion in FY30 requires 9.6% annual growth over the next four years).

The company notes that its strategy of having Salesforce sales reps "sell down" these products, i.e. explaining them to customers in a way they can understand, has helped fuel sales. The company has rebranded all of its core product lines as AI and agentic-capable. Per CEO Marc Benioff's remarks on the Q3 earnings call:

Agentforce and Data reached nearly $1.4 billion in ARR in the quarter, up 114% year-over-year, including Agentforce ARR of about $540 million, 330% year-over-year. And I think all of our account executives, I think we got about 15,000, I don't know what the exact number is out there, are all selling this down, where people really can understand it. We can demo it, we can show it [...]

And every Salesforce app now, not just sales, service, marketing, commerce, all of them, Tableau, Slack, our new ITSM, supply chain products, they've all been rebuilt, and Srini is here, he's going to talk about what we've done to bring Agentforce into every product we have. And they -- we transform Agentforce from being a product to a platform so that all of our apps can reason, learn, take action, collaborate with users, but it's really about humans and apps and the AI and the data all working together.

And unlike the scores of tech companies that are sacrificing profitability to invest in AI, Salesforce continues to expand with efficiency in mind. Pro forma operating margins in Q3 vaulted up 240bps yesterday/y to 35.5%.

On top of a 9% revenue growth rate, Salesforce easily clears the Rule of 40.

Valuation and key takeaways

And yet, despite its strong growth/profitability balance, the stock continues to trade at very discounted valuation multiples. At current share prices near $240, Salesforce trades at a market cap of $225.87 billion. Netting off the $11.32 billion of cash and $8.44 billion of debt on the company's latest balance sheet yields an enterprise value of $222.99 billion.

For the remainder of FY26 (which ends in January 2026), Salesforce has lifted its revenue guidance to $41.45-$41.55 billion, or 9-10% y/y growth. It's also now expecting 13-14% FCF growth, which implies $14.11 billion at the midpoint, or 34% FCF margins, up one point from 33% in FY25.

For next year FY27, Wall Street is expecting Salesforce to deliver $13.11 in pro forma EPS (+11% y/y) on $46.0 billion in revenue, also at 11% y/y growth. If we assume the same trend of 1 point of FCF margin expansion (to a 35% margin), FCF would be $16.10 billion (+14% y/y). This positions Salesforce's valuation multiples at:

  • 4.8x EV/FY27 revenue
  • 13.8x EV/FY27 FCF
  • 18.4x FY27 P/E

For a company with bona fide AI tailwinds, continued margin expansion, and a broad product portfolio, Salesforce remains a great "growth at a reasonable price" play; and in an expensive stock market this can even be considered a bargain. Don't hesitate to load up on this stock at current prices.
